title: White nougat
subtitle: Italian-style egg white nougat with honey, almonds, and pistachios
excerpt: Honey and sugar cooked to hard-ball stage and streamed into whipped egg whites to make a firm, chewy nougat studded with toasted almonds and pistachios. Requires two simultaneous operations and precise temperature work.
sourceNotes: Italian torrone and French nougat de Montélimar are the canonical forms of egg-white nougat stabilised by streaming cooked sugar into whipped whites (Italian meringue method). The technique is documented in French confectionery manuals from the seventeenth century. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), gives a version using honey as the primary sugar. The precise temperature requirements (honey to 121°C, sugar to 135°C) are stable across historical and modern sources.

recipe.yieldDescription: about 600 g (yields a 20 cm x 15 cm slab, 1.5 cm thick)
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps wrapped in baking paper in an airtight tin for 2 weeks at room temperature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A stable meringue made by streaming hot sugar syrup cooked to firm-ball or hard-ball stage (121-135°C) into whipped egg whites while the mixer runs. The heat cooks the whites, producing a dense, glossy, stable foam that holds its structure without baking. Used as the base for nougat, Italian buttercream, and marshmallow.

content.text: White nougat is made by the

method: honey is cooked to 121°C and streamed into whipped egg whites, then sugar syrup is cooked to 135°C and streamed in as a second addition. The two-stage approach gives control over the final texture: the honey addition stabilises the whites, the sugar syrup firms the structure. Nuts go in last, when the nougat is thick enough to hold them suspended.

You are streaming 121-135°C syrup near a running mixer. The risks are a splash burn and a syrup-covered whisk. Stream the syrup down the side of the bowl, not onto the whisk. Keep children away. If hot syrup contacts skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes.

attrs.prepNote: from large eggs, at room temperature

content.text: Cook the honey, whip the whites

Line a 20 cm x 15 cm tin with baking paper. Warm the toasted {{blanched-almonds}} in a low oven (100°C) so they don't cool the nougat when added. Put the {{runny-honey}} in a small saucepan and cook over medium heat to 121°C (firm-ball stage). While the honey cooks, whip the {{egg-whites}} in a stand mixer to stiff peaks. When the honey reaches 121°C, stream it slowly down the side of the bowl into the running mixer. Beat on high for 2 minutes.

Put the {{caster-sugar}}, {{liquid-glucose}}, and 75 ml of water in a separate saucepan. Stir over low heat until dissolved, then cook without stirring to 135°C (hard-ball stage). Stream this syrup slowly down the side of the mixer bowl into the running meringue. Beat on high for 5-8 minutes until the nougat is very thick, holds its shape on the whisk, and has cooled to around 60°C.

Fold in the warm {{blanched-almonds}} and {{shelled-pistachios}} by hand. The nougat will be very stiff. Press into the lined tin and smooth the surface with a wetted spatula. Lay a sheet of baking paper on top and press firmly. Leave at room temperature for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight, until fully set. Cut into bars with a sharp, lightly oiled knife.
